one_line_takeaway: An adversarial LLM serious game (players socially-engineer a GPT-4o 'guardian' into leaking private data) that, via a 22-player qualitative study, catalogs the deceptive linguistic strategies people use against conversational agents and shows the experience raises privacy-vulnerability awareness - a privacy-education contribution, not a new attack or defense.
key_finding: Players spontaneously produced a rich repertoire of deceptive linguistic strategies to extract information from the LLM, and post-play interviews showed they mapped these in-game tactics onto real-world privacy risks (phishing, elderly-targeting scams, data breaches) and expressed concrete intentions to change behavior (stronger passwords, less oversharing with AI, wariness of browser-saved credentials). Coders identified 4 strategy categories and 12 strategies; 'Direct Response' was the most-used category (21 players, 160 uses) and 'make up personal stories' the single most-used strategy (19 players, 77 uses). A striking secondary finding is that emotional rapport induced disclosure even with roles reversed (player-as-attacker), echoing prior work where emotionally-supportive agents made humans over-disclose; and several players realized the fictional cover-stories they invented leaked their own real personal details, making them more privacy-cautious.
core_contribution: The paper presents Cracking Aegis, a dialogue-based 'serious game' that teaches privacy-protection awareness by inverting the usual defender framing: the player is cast as an attacker who impersonates a missing scientist (Dr. Evelyn Smith) and must socially-engineer an LLM-driven AI guardian named Aegis into leaking sensitive data (passwords, locations, surveillance footage, biometric records). The contribution is a design artifact plus a qualitative user study (n=22), not a new attack or defense. Specifically it offers: (1) an adversarial LLM-dialogue game mechanic grounded in privacy literature, mapping the CBGD serious-game framework, Solove's privacy taxonomy, and GDPR data-sensitivity tiers onto six escalating scenarios (Information Collection -> Processing -> Dissemination -> phishing decision -> Invasion/surveillance -> a final ethical data-handling choice); (2) an empirically-derived taxonomy of the deceptive linguistic strategies real players spontaneously used to manipulate the LLM (four categories, twelve strategies); and (3) design guidance for building educational adversarial human-LLM experiences (iterative prompt engineering, ethical framing to avoid normalizing attacker behavior). NOTE: prior systems Hacc-Man and Adversarial Taboo are cited only to contrast against, not as this paper's own work.
threat_model: The game dramatizes real-world social-engineering / phishing threats against anthropomorphic LLM agents rather than proposing or evaluating a novel technical attack. The illustrated threat is linguistic manipulation: an adversary who impersonates a trusted identity and uses storytelling, emotional rapport, urgency, and coercion to induce an LLM agent (or a human) into disclosing private information. The paper explicitly links this to phishing, identity theft, weak/multi-factor authentication bypass, and oversharing of personal data with AI systems. Aegis is a scripted GPT-4o 'guardian' designed to resist casual questioning; it is a pedagogical target, not a hardened production system, and the study does not measure real attack success rates or defensive robustness. So the security relevance is genuine (privacy leakage via social engineering of conversational agents) but the paper's stance is educational/HCI, not offensive-security evaluation.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) low participant diversity - 81.8% Chinese speakers - limiting generalizability, plus possible reduced immersion for non-native English speakers; (2) no quantitative data - n=22 supports qualitative but not statistical inference; no effect sizes or power analysis; (3) single-condition design with no control/between-subjects comparison, so game effects cannot be causally isolated; (4) uncontrolled number of interactions and time-on-task, introducing variability in learning outcomes; (5) LLM randomness meant the effectiveness of each linguistic strategy was NOT analyzed, only its frequency; (6) the end decision point did not fully assess privacy attitudes; (7) implementation variability from prompt engineering (difficulty fluctuations, occasional gameplay-flow breakdowns - e.g. P22 was pushed off the intended flow and never reached the final decision). Reviewer-added: reported behavior change is self-reported intention immediately post-game with no follow-up, so durable behavioral impact is unestablished.

datasets_used: No external/public datasets. Self-collected study data only: in-game text-input logs from 22 participants plus per-scenario and post-game semi-structured interview transcripts.
Participant pool: 61 responded, 22 completed with full data. Demographics - 81.8% Chinese, 9.1% British, 4.5% Tongan, 4.5% Indian; 63.6% aged 18-24, 18.2% aged 25-30; 72.7% female, 27.3% male.
quantitative_results: 21 of 22 participants completed the full game; average of 34 dialogue rounds per player (P22 completed 5/6 scenarios but was knocked off the intended flow before the final decision).
Strategy category 'Direct Response' = most-used category: 21 players, 160 total uses.
'Make up personal stories' = most-used single strategy of the 12: 19 players, 77 uses (P14 used it 9 times).
'Issue direct commands or requests': 20 players, 70 uses.
'Fabricate false information': 14 players, 66 uses; heavy users P5/P15/P22 at 8/8/9 uses.
'Establish emotional connections': 15 players; 'Feign vulnerability': 10 players; 'Use praise and flattery': 6 players.
'Engage in questioning/counter-questioning': 11 players (one used it 7 times); 'Use bribery and temptation': rare, used only 3 times, all by P5.
Phishing-email decision point: 8 players refused to click the link (P1,P2,P3,P10,P15,P17,P18,P19); 14 clicked it (P4-P9,P11-P14,P16,P20,P21,P22).

Final biometric-data decision (21 players reached it; P22 never did): keep hidden (8: P1,P3,P5,P9,P10,P13,P15,P20), destroy (7: P2,P4,P6,P7,P8,P11,P16), expose publicly (3: P12,P18,P21), share with authorities (3: P14,P17,P19).
baselines_compared: None empirically. This is a single-condition qualitative study with no control group or competing system evaluated.

Conceptual (not experimental) contrasts drawn against prior LLM games: Hacc-Man (discrete jailbreak challenges) and Adversarial Taboo (attacker/defender NLP benchmark, not educational), positioned as the closest related work that Cracking Aegis extends via literature-grounded scenario design.
